summary: This brief post promotes O'Neil's Bloomberg column arguing that existing crime risk score data can be repurposed to guide police defunding efforts. She suggests that the same algorithmic tools used for predictive policing inadvertently reveal which social problems have been inappropriately delegated to law enforcement. The post positions this as making defunding practically straightforward since the analytical groundwork already exists.
key_points: Crime risk scores, originally designed for predictive policing, actually predict police presence rather than crime itself
This existing data can be repurposed to identify where police are handling problems better suited to other social services
The analytical infrastructure for defunding already exists - it just needs to be reframed
money_quotes: Crime-risk scores predict police, and not in a good way
Crime-risk scores reveal the problems that society has shunted onto law enforcement
key_insight: The same predictive policing algorithms that critics have long attacked for bias can now serve as a roadmap for identifying which police functions should be transferred to social services.
